claims.claim_text: `securityContext` can be set at both Pod and container level, and container-level settings override overlapping Pod-level settings.
claims.span_hint: section: SecurityContext: The Main Control Surface
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes security context task explicitly states that container security settings override Pod-level settings when there is overlap.

claims.claim_text: `fsGroup`-driven ownership changes can slow Pod startup for large volumes, which is why volume permission policy matters.
claims.span_hint: section: SecurityContext: The Main Control Surface | table row for `fsGroup`
claims.rationale: The security context documentation says Kubernetes recursively changes ownership for `fsGroup` mounts and that on large volumes this can take a lot of time, slowing Pod startup.

claims.claim_text: Binding to ports below 1024 is controlled by the Linux capability `NET_BIND_SERVICE`, so that need does not justify `privileged: true`.
claims.span_hint: section: Pod Security Starts With Boundaries | paragraph after the 'Stop and think' callout
claims.rationale: This is a real Linux capability fact, but the clean primary source is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: A Kubernetes Service can expose port 80 while sending traffic to a different higher `targetPort` on the Pod.
claims.span_hint: section: Pod Security Starts With Boundaries | paragraph beginning 'A Service can expose port 80...'
claims.rationale: The Service documentation explicitly shows `port` and `targetPort` mapping and notes that a Service can map any incoming port to a targetPort.

claims.claim_text: Linux capabilities let a process receive some privileges without receiving all root privileges, and Kubernetes lets you add or drop them via `securityContext`.
claims.span_hint: section: Linux Capabilities: Smaller Than Root, Still Powerful
claims.rationale: The security context documentation states that Linux capabilities grant certain privileges without all root privileges and are configured in `securityContext`.

claims.claim_text: Privileged containers override seccomp, AppArmor, and SELinux constraints and are given all Linux capabilities.
claims.span_hint: section: Privileged Containers: Treat Them as Node-Trust Decisions
claims.rationale: The Linux kernel security constraints page explicitly says privileged containers run unconfined for seccomp, ignore AppArmor and SELinux constraints, and are given all Linux capabilities.

claims.claim_text: `allowPrivilegeEscalation` controls whether a process can gain more privileges than its parent, and it is effectively always true for privileged containers or containers with `CAP_SYS_ADMIN`.
claims.span_hint: section: Seccomp and Privilege Escalation: Runtime Guardrails
claims.rationale: The security context documentation defines `allowPrivilegeEscalation` and explicitly says it is always true when the container is privileged or has `CAP_SYS_ADMIN`.

claims.claim_text: Pod Security Standards define three cumulative profiles: `privileged`, `baseline`, and `restricted`.
claims.span_hint: section: Pod Security Standards: Three Profiles, Different Purposes
claims.rationale: The Pod Security Standards page says the standards define three policies and that the policies are cumulative.

claims.claim_text: The `baseline` profile is minimally restrictive and is intended to prevent known privilege escalations while allowing the default minimally specified Pod configuration.
claims.span_hint: section: Pod Security Standards: Three Profiles, Different Purposes
claims.rationale: The PSS profile table and Baseline section describe Baseline as minimally restrictive and intended to prevent known privilege escalations.

claims.claim_text: The `restricted` profile is a more heavily locked-down profile aligned with current Pod hardening best practices.
claims.span_hint: section: Pod Security Standards: Three Profiles, Different Purposes
claims.rationale: The PSS profile table describes Restricted as a heavily restricted policy following current Pod hardening best practices.

claims.claim_text: Baseline disallows sharing host namespaces via `hostNetwork`, `hostPID`, and `hostIPC`.
claims.span_hint: section: Host Namespaces: Where Isolation Can Disappear
claims.rationale: The Baseline policy specification explicitly lists `spec.hostNetwork`, `spec.hostPID`, and `spec.hostIPC` as disallowed host namespace sharing controls.

claims.claim_text: Baseline disallows privileged containers and forbids `hostPath` volumes.
claims.span_hint: section: Host Namespaces: Where Isolation Can Disappear | table row for `hostPath`; section: Pod Security Standards
claims.rationale: The Baseline policy specification says privileged containers must be disallowed and `hostPath` volumes must be forbidden.

claims.claim_text: Baseline only permits a limited capability add allowlist rather than arbitrary added Linux capabilities.
claims.span_hint: section: Pod Security Standards: Three Profiles, Different Purposes | table row 'Dangerous capabilities'
claims.rationale: The Baseline capability rules explicitly restrict `capabilities.add` to a defined allowlist rather than arbitrary capability additions.

claims.claim_text: Restricted requires `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false` for containers.
claims.span_hint: section: Pod Security Standards: Three Profiles, Different Purposes | table row for `allowPrivilegeEscalation`; section: Seccomp and Privilege Escalation
claims.rationale: The Restricted PSS requirements explicitly list `allowPrivilegeEscalation` with the allowed value `false`.

claims.claim_text: Restricted requires non-root behavior by requiring `runAsNonRoot: true` and forbidding `runAsUser: 0`.
claims.span_hint: section: Pod Security Standards: Three Profiles, Different Purposes | table row 'Running as UID 0'; quiz and worked examples discussing root under restricted
claims.rationale: The Restricted PSS rules require `runAsNonRoot` true and state that containers must not set `runAsUser` to 0.

claims.claim_text: Restricted requires seccomp to be explicitly set and forbids both a missing profile and the `Unconfined` profile.
claims.span_hint: section: Pod Security Standards: Three Profiles, Different Purposes | table row 'Missing seccomp profile'; section: Seccomp and Privilege Escalation
claims.rationale: The Restricted PSS rules say seccomp must be explicitly set to an allowed value and that both absence and `Unconfined` are prohibited.

claims.claim_text: Restricted requires containers to drop `ALL` capabilities and only permits adding back `NET_BIND_SERVICE`.
claims.span_hint: section: Pod Security Standards: Three Profiles, Different Purposes | table row for dangerous capabilities; section: Linux Capabilities
claims.rationale: The Restricted capability rules explicitly require dropping `ALL` and only allow `NET_BIND_SERVICE` as an add-back capability.

claims.claim_text: Valid seccomp profile types in Kubernetes are `RuntimeDefault`, `Unconfined`, and `Localhost`.
claims.span_hint: section: Seccomp and Privilege Escalation: Runtime Guardrails
claims.rationale: The security context documentation lists `RuntimeDefault`, `Unconfined`, and `Localhost` as the valid seccomp profile types.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes recommends using the container runtime's default seccomp profile for most workloads.
claims.span_hint: section: SecurityContext: The Main Control Surface | paragraph on `RuntimeDefault`; section: Seccomp and Privilege Escalation
claims.rationale: The Linux kernel security constraints page recommends using the default seccomp profile bundled with the container runtime.

claims.claim_text: Pod Security Admission is the built-in Kubernetes admission controller that enforces Pod Security Standards at the namespace level.
claims.span_hint: section: Pod Security Admission: Enforcing Standards With Namespace Labels
claims.rationale: The PSA documentation says Kubernetes offers a built-in Pod Security admission controller and that pod security restrictions are applied at the namespace level.

claims.claim_text: PSA modes behave differently: `enforce` rejects violating pods, `warn` returns user-facing warnings, and `audit` records audit-log annotations; per-mode version labels accept either a Kubernetes minor version or `latest`.
claims.span_hint: section: Pod Security Admission: Enforcing Standards With Namespace Labels
claims.rationale: The PSA documentation defines `enforce`, `warn`, and `audit`, and documents per-mode `*-version` labels that can be a minor version or `latest`.

claims.claim_text: A pod running as UID 0 can still pass `baseline` but trigger `restricted` warnings if it avoids the fields that baseline blocks.
claims.span_hint: section: Pod Security Admission: Enforcing Standards With Namespace Labels | paragraph beginning 'Now imagine a developer submits a pod that runs as root...'
claims.rationale: This is a direct inference from PSA mode semantics plus PSS rules: Baseline does not require non-root, while Restricted does.

claims.claim_text: PodSecurityPolicy was deprecated in Kubernetes v1.21, removed in v1.25, and Kubernetes directs users to Pod Security Admission or third-party admission plugins instead.
claims.span_hint: section: Pod Security Admission: Enforcing Standards With Namespace Labels | sentence describing PSA as replacement for PodSecurityPolicy; section: Did You Know?
claims.rationale: The PodSecurityPolicy documentation explicitly marks PSP as deprecated in v1.21, removed in v1.25, and points readers to PSA and third-party admission plugins.

claims.claim_text: Container image tags are mutable, while image digests are fixed; using a digest is more predictable than `:latest`.
claims.span_hint: section: Hands-On Exercise: Security Analysis and Remediation | Step 1 table row for `image: myapp:latest`
claims.rationale: The images documentation says tags can be moved to point to different images, while digests are fixed and uniquely identify a specific version.

further_reading.title: Pod Security Standards
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the authoritative definition of what `privileged`, `baseline`, and `restricted` actually allow and forbid.

further_reading.title: Pod Security Admission
further_reading.why_relevant: This explains how namespace labels, PSA modes, and version pinning translate the standards into admission behavior.

further_reading.title: Linux Kernel Security Constraints for Pods and Containers
further_reading.why_relevant: This connects Kubernetes pod fields to the underlying Linux isolation mechanisms such as seccomp, capabilities, and privileged mode.

notes: No rewrite dispositions were needed. `source_ids` are left empty because no shared section pool was provided; each claim carries its own proposed URL.
